Flicker Noise Model

hydra_tod.flicker_model.my_gamma_inc(z: complex | float, R_vals: float | ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]], epsabs: float = 1e-06, epsrel: float = 1e-06, para_run: bool = True) → complex | ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[complexfloating]][source]

Compute the upper incomplete gamma function via line-integral representation.

Evaluates \(\Gamma(z, iR)\) by numerically integrating along the contour \(s = iR + t\) for \(t \in [0, \infty)\):

\[\Gamma(z, iR) = \int_0^\infty e^{-(iR + t)} (iR + t)^{z-1} \, dt\]
Parameters:

  • z (complex or float) – Complex (or real) order of the incomplete gamma function.

  • R_vals (float or array_like) – Positive real shift(s) defining the imaginary starting point of the contour. Must be strictly positive.

  • epsabs (float, optional) – Absolute error tolerance for scipy.integrate.quad. Default is 1e-6.

  • epsrel (float, optional) – Relative error tolerance for scipy.integrate.quad. Default is 1e-6.

  • para_run (bool, optional) – If True (default), evaluate entries in parallel using mpiutil.local_parallel_func. Otherwise, use np.vectorize.

Returns:

Result with the same shape as R_vals. Scalar input yields a scalar output.

Return type:

complex or NDArray[np.complexfloating]

Notes

This is a numerically robust alternative to mpmath.gammainc for moderate-precision requirements where vectorised evaluation is needed.

References

Zhang et al. (2026), RASTI, rzag024.

hydra_tod.flicker_model.aux_int_v1(mu: float, u: float) → float[source]

Evaluate the auxiliary integral for the flicker correlation at a single lag.

Computes:

\[\operatorname{Re}\!\bigl[e^{-i\pi\mu/2}\,\Gamma(\mu, iu)\bigr] = \operatorname{Re}(\Gamma(\mu, iu))\cos\!\bigl(\tfrac{\pi}{2}\mu\bigr) + \operatorname{Im}(\Gamma(\mu, iu))\sin\!\bigl(\tfrac{\pi}{2}\mu\bigr)\]

Uses mpmath.gammainc for arbitrary-precision evaluation.

Parameters:

  • mu (float) – Order parameter, typically \(\mu = 1 - \alpha\).

  • u (float) – Dimensionless lag argument \(u = \omega_c \tau\).

Returns:

Real-valued result of the auxiliary integral.

Return type:

float

hydra_tod.flicker_model.aux_int_v2(mu: float, u_list: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]]) → ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]][source]

Vectorised auxiliary integral for the flicker correlation function.

Computes the same quantity as aux_int_v1() but for an array of lag arguments, using my_gamma_inc() for parallel evaluation.

Parameters:

  • mu (float) – Order parameter, typically \(\mu = 1 - \alpha\).

  • u_list (NDArray[np.floating]) – Array of dimensionless lag arguments \(u = \omega_c \tau\).

Returns:

Real-valued results with the same shape as u_list.

Return type:

NDArray[np.float64]

hydra_tod.flicker_model.aux_calculation(u: float, mu: float) → float[source]

Compute the auxiliary integral (single-lag, argument-order swapped).

Identical to aux_int_v1() but with swapped argument order for compatibility with legacy call sites.

Parameters:

  • u (float) – Dimensionless lag argument \(u = \omega_c \tau\).

  • mu (float) – Order parameter, typically \(\mu = 1 - \alpha\).

Returns:

Real-valued result of the auxiliary integral.

Return type:

float

hydra_tod.flicker_model.flicker_corr(tau: float, f0: float, fc: float, alpha: float, var_w: float = 0.0) → float[source]

Compute the flicker-noise correlation at a single time lag.

Evaluates the analytic covariance function of \(1/f^\alpha\) noise with a low-frequency cutoff:

\[C(\tau) = \frac{(\omega_0 \tau)^\alpha}{\pi \tau} \operatorname{Re}\!\bigl[e^{-i\pi\mu/2}\, \Gamma(\mu, i\omega_c\tau)\bigr], \quad \mu = 1 - \alpha\]

At zero lag (\(\tau = 0\)):

\[C(0) = \frac{\omega_c}{\pi} \left(\frac{\omega_0}{\omega_c}\right)^\alpha \frac{1}{\alpha - 1} + \sigma_w^2\]
Parameters:

  • tau (float) – Time lag in seconds.

  • f0 (float) – Knee angular frequency \(\omega_0 = 2\pi f_{\mathrm{knee}}\) (rad/s).

  • fc (float) – Cutoff angular frequency \(\omega_c = 2\pi / (N \Delta t)\) (rad/s).

  • alpha (float) – Spectral index of the \(1/f^\alpha\) noise (\(\alpha > 1\)).

  • var_w (float, optional) – White-noise variance added at zero lag. Default is 0.0.

Returns:

Correlation value \(C(\tau)\).

Return type:

float

Notes

The angular frequency convention (\(\omega = 2\pi f\)) is used throughout, which differs from the FFT frequency convention by a factor of \(2\pi\).

References

Zhang et al. (2026), RASTI, rzag024.

hydra_tod.flicker_model.flicker_corr_vec(taus: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]], f0: float, fc: float, alpha: float) → ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]][source]

Vectorised flicker-noise correlation for an array of non-zero lags.

Computes the same quantity as flicker_corr() but for multiple lags simultaneously, using the vectorised auxiliary integral aux_int_v2().

Parameters:

  • taus (NDArray[np.floating]) – Array of strictly positive time lags (seconds).

  • f0 (float) – Knee angular frequency \(\omega_0\) (rad/s).

  • fc (float) – Cutoff angular frequency \(\omega_c\) (rad/s).

  • alpha (float) – Spectral index (\(\alpha > 1\)).

Returns:

Correlation values for each lag in taus.

Return type:

NDArray[np.float64]

Notes

The angular frequency convention (\(\omega = 2\pi f\)) is used. All elements of taus must be non-zero; use flicker_cov_vec() to include the zero-lag term.

References

Zhang et al. (2026), RASTI, rzag024.

hydra_tod.flicker_model.flicker_cov_vec(tau_list: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]], f0: float, fc: float, alpha: float, white_n_variance: float = 2.5e-06) → ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]][source]

Compute the first row of the flicker covariance matrix (vectorised).

Evaluates the correlation function at all lags in tau_list, handling the zero-lag term separately (analytic expression plus white-noise variance).

Parameters:

  • tau_list (NDArray[np.floating]) – Array of time lags starting with 0. Typically produced by hydra_tod.utils.lag_list().

  • f0 (float) – Knee angular frequency \(\omega_0\) (rad/s).

  • fc (float) – Cutoff angular frequency \(\omega_c\) (rad/s).

  • alpha (float) – Spectral index (\(\alpha > 1\)).

  • white_n_variance (float, optional) – White-noise variance \(\sigma_w^2\) added at zero lag. Default is 2.5e-6.

Returns:

Correlation vector (first row of the Toeplitz covariance matrix).

Return type:

NDArray[np.float64]

Raises:

AssertionError – If tau_list[0] != 0.

References

Zhang et al. (2026), RASTI, rzag024.

hydra_tod.flicker_model.flicker_cov(time_list: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]], f0: float, fc: float, alpha: float, white_n_variance: float = 5e-06, only_row_0: bool = False) → ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]][source]

Construct the Toeplitz covariance matrix of flicker noise.

Computes the correlation at each unique lag from time_list and assembles the full symmetric Toeplitz matrix, or optionally returns only the first row.

Parameters:

  • time_list (NDArray[np.floating]) – Observation time stamps (seconds). The lag list is derived via hydra_tod.utils.lag_list().

  • f0 (float) – Knee angular frequency \(\omega_0\) (rad/s).

  • fc (float) – Cutoff angular frequency \(\omega_c\) (rad/s).

  • alpha (float) – Spectral index (\(\alpha > 1\)).

  • white_n_variance (float, optional) – White-noise variance \(\sigma_w^2\). Default is 5e-6.

  • only_row_0 (bool, optional) – If True, return only the first row of the Toeplitz matrix. Default is False.

Returns:

If only_row_0 is False, the full \(N \times N\) Toeplitz covariance matrix. Otherwise, a 1-D array of length \(N\).

Return type:

NDArray[np.float64]

Notes

The matrix is constructed via scipy.linalg.toeplitz from the correlation vector. For large \(N\), consider using FlickerCorrEmulator for faster evaluation.

References

Zhang et al. (2026), RASTI, rzag024.

hydra_tod.flicker_model.flicker_corr_full(tau: float, f0: float, fc: float, alpha: float, var_w: float = 0.0) → float[source]

Compute the flicker-noise correlation including the DC mode contribution.

Extends flicker_corr() with an additional sinusoidal term that accounts for non-vanishing power at the DC mode and its adjacent frequencies:

\[C_{\mathrm{full}}(\tau) = \frac{1}{\pi\tau}\Bigl[ (\omega_0\tau)^\alpha\,\operatorname{Re}[e^{-i\pi\mu/2}\Gamma(\mu,i\omega_c\tau)] + \bigl(\omega_0/\omega_c\bigr)^\alpha\sin(\omega_c\tau) \Bigr]\]
Parameters:

  • tau (float) – Time lag in seconds.

  • f0 (float) – Knee angular frequency \(\omega_0\) (rad/s).

  • fc (float) – Cutoff angular frequency \(\omega_c\) (rad/s).

  • alpha (float) – Spectral index (\(\alpha > 1\)).

  • var_w (float, optional) – White-noise variance added at zero lag. Default is 0.0.

Returns:

Correlation value including the DC contribution.

Return type:

float

References

Zhang et al. (2026), RASTI, rzag024.

hydra_tod.flicker_model.sim_noise(f0: float, fc: float, alpha: float, time_list: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]], n_samples: int = 1, white_n_variance: float = 5e-06) → ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]][source]

Generate realisations of correlated flicker noise.

Constructs the full Toeplitz covariance matrix from the analytic correlation function and draws samples from the corresponding multivariate normal distribution.

Parameters:

  • f0 (float) – Knee angular frequency \(\omega_0\) (rad/s).

  • fc (float) – Cutoff angular frequency \(\omega_c\) (rad/s).

  • alpha (float) – Spectral index (\(\alpha > 1\)).

  • time_list (NDArray[np.floating]) – Observation time stamps (seconds).

  • n_samples (int, optional) – Number of independent noise realisations. Default is 1.

  • white_n_variance (float, optional) – White-noise variance \(\sigma_w^2\). Default is 5e-6.

Returns:

If n_samples == 1, a 1-D array of length len(time_list). Otherwise, a 2-D array of shape (n_samples, len(time_list)).

Return type:

NDArray[np.float64]

Notes

This function builds the dense covariance matrix and is therefore \(O(N^2)\) in memory and \(O(N^3)\) in compute due to the Cholesky decomposition inside np.random.multivariate_normal.

References

Zhang et al. (2026), RASTI, rzag024.

hydra_tod.flicker_model.flicker_cov_general(time_list: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]], f0: float, fc: float, alpha: float, white_n_variance: float = 5e-06) → ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]][source]

Construct the full covariance matrix for arbitrary (non-consecutive) time stamps.

Unlike flicker_cov(), which assumes evenly-spaced times and builds a Toeplitz matrix from the first-row correlation vector, this function computes all pairwise time differences and evaluates the correlation function at each unique lag. The result is a general symmetric positive- definite matrix suitable for flagged / non-contiguous time samples.

Parameters:

  • time_list (NDArray[np.floating]) – Observation time stamps (seconds), not necessarily evenly spaced.

  • f0 (float) – Knee angular frequency \(\omega_0\) (rad/s).

  • fc (float) – Cutoff angular frequency \(\omega_c\) (rad/s).

  • alpha (float) – Spectral index (\(\alpha > 1\)).

  • white_n_variance (float, optional) – White-noise variance \(\sigma_w^2\) added to the diagonal. Default is 5e-6.

Returns:

Full \(N \times N\) covariance matrix.

Return type:

NDArray[np.float64]

Notes

For consecutive (evenly-spaced) time stamps, prefer flicker_cov() which exploits the Toeplitz structure for faster evaluation.

References

Zhang et al. (2026), RASTI, rzag024.

class hydra_tod.flicker_model.FNoise_traditional(alpha: float, dtime: float = 2, dnu: float = 200000.0, fknee: float = 0.001)[source]

Bases: object

Traditional \(1/f^\alpha\) noise generator using FFT filtering.

Generates time-domain noise realisations by shaping white noise in the Fourier domain with a \(1/f^\alpha\) power spectral density (PSD). The white-noise variance is set by the radiometer equation:

\[\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{\Delta t \, \Delta\nu}\]

where \(\Delta t\) is the integration time and \(\Delta\nu\) is the frequency channel width.

Parameters:

  • alpha (float) – Spectral index of the \(1/f^\alpha\) noise.

  • dtime (float, optional) – Sampling / integration time per data point (seconds). Default is 2.

  • dnu (float, optional) – Frequency channel width (Hz). Default is 2e5.

  • fknee (float, optional) – Knee angular frequency \(\omega_{\mathrm{knee}} = 2\pi f_{\mathrm{knee}}\) (rad/s). Default is 0.001.

sigma_2

White-noise variance \(1/(\Delta t \, \Delta\nu)\).

Type:

float

References

Zhang et al. (2026), RASTI, rzag024.

generate(ntime: int) → ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[float64]][source]

Generate a single realisation of \(1/f^\alpha\) noise.

Parameters:

ntime (int) – Number of time samples.

Returns:

Time-domain noise array of length ntime.

Return type:

NDArray[np.float64]

Notes

The procedure is:

  1. Compute the FFT frequency axis and convert to angular frequency.

  2. Build the PSD filter \(\sqrt{(\omega_{\mathrm{knee}}/|\omega|)^\alpha}\).

  3. Generate white noise, FFT, multiply by the filter, and IFFT.

class hydra_tod.flicker_model.FlickerCorrEmulator(logfc: float, tau_list: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]], wnoise_var: float = 2.5e-06, alpha_training: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]] | None = None, corr_training: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]] | None = None, alpha_test: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]] | None = None, corr_test: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]] | None = None)[source]

Bases: object

Polynomial emulator for the flicker-noise correlation function.

Trains a MomentEmu.PolyEmu polynomial emulator to approximate flicker_cov_vec() as a function of the spectral index \(\alpha\), enabling fast evaluation without repeated numerical integration.

The emulator is split into two parts:

  • Auto-correlation (small lags, indices 0:5): emulates \(\ln C(\tau)\) to preserve positivity.

  • Cross-correlation (remaining lags): emulates \(C(\tau)\) directly.

Scaling with \(\omega_0\) is handled analytically:

\[C(\tau;\,\omega_0,\alpha) = 10^{(\log_{10}\omega_0 - \log_{10}\omega_0^{\mathrm{ref}})\,\alpha} \; C(\tau;\,\omega_0^{\mathrm{ref}},\alpha)\]
Parameters:

  • logfc (float) – Log10 of the cutoff angular frequency \(\log_{10}\omega_c\).

  • tau_list (NDArray[np.floating]) – Array of time lags (seconds), starting with zero.

  • wnoise_var (float, optional) – White-noise variance added at zero lag. Default is 2.5e-6.

  • alpha_training (NDArray[np.floating] or None, optional) – Training values of \(\alpha\). Default generates 3000 points linearly spaced in [1.1, 4].

  • corr_training (NDArray[np.floating] or None, optional) – Pre-computed training correlation vectors. If None, computed from alpha_training via flicker_cov_vec().

  • alpha_test (NDArray[np.floating] or None, optional) – Test values of \(\alpha\). Default generates 500 uniform random points in [1.1, 4].

  • corr_test (NDArray[np.floating] or None, optional) – Pre-computed test correlation vectors.

ref_logf0

Reference \(\log_{10}\omega_0\) used for training (-4.0).

Type:

float

lag_emulator

Emulator for lags with index >= 5.

Type:

PolyEmu

auto_emulator

Emulator for lags with index < 5 (log-space).

Type:

PolyEmu

References

Zhang et al. (2026), RASTI, rzag024.

create_jax() → Callable[source]

Create a JAX-autodifferentiable version of the emulator.

Returns:

A JAX-compatible function with signature (logf0, alpha, indices=None) -> jnp.ndarray.

Return type:

Callable

Notes

Requires JAX and MomentEmu.jax_momentemu. The returned function is suitable for use with jax.jit and jax.grad.

class hydra_tod.flicker_model.LogDetEmulator(params_list: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]], log_det_list: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]], X_test: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]], Y_test: ndarray[tuple[int, ...], dtype[floating]])[source]

Bases: object

Polynomial emulator for the log-determinant of the flicker covariance.

Trains a MomentEmu.PolyEmu emulator to approximate \(\ln\det\mathbf{C}(\log_{10}\omega_0, \alpha)\), enabling fast evaluation of the Gaussian log-likelihood normalisation term during MCMC sampling.

Parameters:

  • params_list (NDArray[np.floating]) – Training parameter array of shape (n_train, 2) with columns [logf0, alpha].

  • log_det_list (NDArray[np.floating]) – Corresponding log-determinant values, shape (n_train, 1).

  • X_test (NDArray[np.floating]) – Test parameter array, shape (n_test, 2).

  • Y_test (NDArray[np.floating]) – Test log-determinant values, shape (n_test, 1).

References

Zhang et al. (2026), RASTI, rzag024.

create_jax() → Callable[source]

Create a JAX-autodifferentiable version of the log-det emulator.

Returns:

A JAX-compatible function with signature (logf0, alpha) -> jnp.ndarray.

Return type:

Callable
